name: Marker boards
blurb: Mast and lineside plates that tell you how to read the signal they mark.
intro: German marker boards (Signaltafeln) and mast plates (Mastschilder) stand on or beside the signal post to tell you what kind of signal you are looking at, and how to behave if it is at stop or has failed. This family covers the mast plates — from the white-red-white plate that marks an absolute main signal to the banded and dotted plates that let you pass a failed signal under certain rules — the yellow downward triangle that marks a Ks signal with distant function, and the Ne 2 board that announces a distant signal ahead. They carry no speed or stop command of their own; they qualify the signals around them.

variants.name: Main signal mast plate
variants.blurb: White-red-white plate on the mast — this is a main (stop) signal.

aspects.name: Main signal (Hauptsignal)
aspects.meaning: Absolute signal — stop if it is at danger or dark
aspects.whatItMeans: A white-red-white plate on a signal mast marks the signal as a main signal (Hauptsignal). It is an absolute signal: if it is showing stop (Hp 0) or is dark and unreadable, you must stop and may not pass it without authority.
aspects.whatYouDo: Treat a red or dark main signal as stop. Do not pass it without a proceed aspect, a subsidiary/substitute signal (such as Zs 1), or authority from the signaller.
aspects.lookAlikes: The red band is the key. A mast plate without it — or with other markings — can mark a self-acting block signal you may pass at caution when it is dark; the white-red-white plate does not permit that.
aspects.realWorldNote: German signal mast plates (Mastschilder) come in several patterns; white-red-white is the standard absolute main-signal plate. Automatic/self-block signals carry different plates that allow passing at caution when unlit.

variants.name: Main + distant mast plate
variants.blurb: White-yellow-white-yellow-white — a main signal that also acts as a distant signal.

aspects.name: Main signal with distant function
aspects.meaning: Main signal that also announces the next one; a failed one may be passed on sight if the signaller can't be reached
aspects.whatItMeans: A white-yellow-white-yellow-white plate marks a light main signal that also has a distant-signal function — it tells you about the next signal as well as governing its own stop. If it is showing stop or has failed and, after stopping in front of it, you cannot reach the signaller, you may pass without consent and run on sight to the next main signal.
aspects.whatYouDo: Stop at a red or failed signal and try to contact the signaller. If you cannot, pass at caution and run on sight to the next main signal, ready to stop short of any obstruction.
aspects.lookAlikes: The yellow stripes separate it from the white-red-white absolute plate, which never lets you pass a failed signal on your own judgement.
aspects.realWorldNote: Weiß-gelb-weiß-gelb-weiß Mastschild. Marks a Lichthauptsignal that also has Vorsignalfunktion.

variants.name: S-Bahn automatic mast plate
variants.blurb: White-black-white-black-white — Berlin/Hamburg DC S-Bahn automatic block.

aspects.name: S-Bahn automatic block plate
aspects.meaning: Berlin/Hamburg DC S-Bahn — a stop or failed signal may be passed on sight after stopping
aspects.whatItMeans: A white-black-white-black-white plate is used on the Berlin and Hamburg DC S-Bahn. At a signal so marked showing stop or failed, you may pass without consent after stopping in front of it, then run on sight under the S-Bahn's local rules. Such signals also have a distant-signal function.
aspects.whatYouDo: Stop, then pass at caution on sight per the S-Bahn directives, ready to stop.
aspects.realWorldNote: Weiß-schwarz-weiß-schwarz-weiß Mastschild. Berlin (Ril 432 / SBS) and Hamburg S-Bahn only.

variants.name: S-Bahn red mast plate
variants.blurb: All-red plate — Berlin DC S-Bahn; pass a stop signal only on authority.

aspects.name: S-Bahn absolute plate
aspects.meaning: Berlin DC S-Bahn — pass a stop or failed signal only on a substitute signal or written order
aspects.whatItMeans: A plain red plate is used only on the Berlin DC S-Bahn. At a signal so marked showing stop or failed, you may pass only on a substitute signal (Zs 1), a wrong-line substitute (Zs 8), a written order (Befehl), or — at a Zs 12 signal — a verbal instruction.
aspects.whatYouDo: Treat it as absolute. Do not pass without one of the listed authorities.
aspects.realWorldNote: Rotes Mastschild. Berlin DC S-Bahn only (Ril 432).

variants.name: Two-dot mast plate
variants.blurb: White plate with two black dots — pass a stop signal only on written order.

panels.label: White mast plate with two black dots

aspects.name: Two black dots
aspects.meaning: Pass a stop signal only on a written order; dark or Ra 12 means it doesn't apply to trains
aspects.whatItMeans: A white plate with two black dots marks a signal you may pass, when it is at stop, only on a written order (Befehl). If such a signal shows Ra 12 or is extinguished, it has no meaning for trains.
aspects.whatYouDo: Stop at a red signal and wait for a written order before passing. If the signal is dark or shows the shunting-stop-lifted indication, it does not apply to your train.

variants.name: Distant-function plate (Ks)
variants.blurb: Yellow downward triangle below the white-red-white plate — a Ks signal that is main and distant in one.

aspects.name: Ks main signal with distant function
aspects.meaning: A Ks signal that shows both its own aspect and an advance warning of the next signal
aspects.whatItMeans: A yellow triangle pointing downwards, placed below the white-red-white plate on a Ks signal, means that Ks main signal also has a distant-signal function — it shows you the aspect of the next signal as well as governing its own stop. It is combined main-and-distant on a single head.
aspects.whatYouDo: Read a Ks 1 / Ks 2 / Hp 0 here as both a main aspect and an advance warning of the next signal, and brake accordingly.
aspects.sequenceNote: Ks 1 = proceed, Ks 2 = expect stop, Hp 0 = stop. With this plate a green (Ks 1) also promises the next signal is passable; a yellow (Ks 2) warns the next signal is at stop.
aspects.realWorldNote: Gelbes, mit der Spitze nach unten weisendes Mastschild (§ 5a (1a)). Marks a Ks-Hauptsignal mit Vorsignalfunktion, placed below the white-red-white plate.

variants.name: Distant signal board (Ne 2)
variants.blurb: White board with a black diagonal cross (X) — a distant signal is coming.

aspects.name: Distant signal board (Ne 2)
aspects.meaning: A distant signal is to be expected
aspects.whatItMeans: The Ne 2 board — a white board with a black diagonal cross (an X, its arms running corner to corner) — announces that a distant signal (Vorsignal) is to be expected. It can also stand alone to announce a main signal, a line-closing signal, or a Trapeztafel (Ne 1).
aspects.whatYouDo: Be ready to read a distant signal shortly and act on the aspect it shows. The board itself gives no speed or stop command — it is a warning that a signal is coming.
aspects.sequenceNote: On the approach the striped distant-signal beacons (Vorsignalbaken, Ne 3) count you down to the distant signal — three, then two, then one stripe at roughly 250 m, 175 m and 100 m.
aspects.realWorldNote: Ne 2 (Vorsignaltafel). You meet it where a distant signal’s position needs marking; in Train Sim World it warns you a distant signal is near.
